# Abstract Versions

## Short abstract

Digital markets increasingly allocate attention, trust, money, and legitimacy through proxy signals such as views, reviews, ratings, installs, upvotes, follower counts, conversion events, and revenue metrics. This paper argues that unethical growth should be understood as manufactured traction: the strategic distortion, amplification, or concealment of those signals so that platforms, audiences, customers, or investors infer genuine demand where the underlying evidence has been engineered. Drawing on research into manufactured attention, platform-policy documents, consumer-protection guidance, and current case studies across social media, app stores, search, advertising, streaming, reviews, and startup finance, the paper develops a five-layer model of signal corruption: visibility, credibility, conversion, attribution, and legitimacy. It concludes that the ethical harm is systemic: manufactured traction does not merely deceive individual users, but degrades the systems responsible for deciding what deserves attention and trust.

## Longer abstract

The contemporary internet is governed by proxy signals. Platforms rank content through engagement, marketplaces summarize trust through reviews, app stores allocate discovery through ratings and installs, advertisers optimize toward clicks and leads, and investors infer startup viability from simplified metrics such as ARR. This paper argues that a growing family of commercial practices can be understood as manufactured traction: the strategic distortion, amplification, or concealment of visibility, credibility, conversion, attribution, and legitimacy signals. The paper synthesizes platform policy, regulator action, academic work on manufactured attention and recommender systems, and case studies including clipping campaigns, trend simulation, fake reviews, app-store discovery fraud, search spam, paid-ad fraud, mobile attribution fraud, streaming fraud, dark patterns, and startup metric inflation. It distinguishes legitimate growth from manipulative growth by asking whether a tactic improves truthful discoverability or falsifies the evidence of demand. The paper proposes an evidence ladder and detection framework for studying these practices without collapsing all marketing into deception. It concludes that manufactured traction is a systemic threat because it corrupts the signals digital markets use to allocate attention, trust, money, and legitimacy.
